source.title: BSEE Accident Investigation Report - Structural Damage Crane Other Lifting

actions_taken: BOP/LMRP recovery operations initiated on December 2, 2021
Seafloor jetting performed to break suction effect
Spear assembly applied 980,000 lbs of vertical pull
BOP/LMRP recovered to surface on December 18, 2021
event.summary: On November 20, 2021, at 02:15 hours, the riser system and subsea blowout preventer (BOP/lower marine riser package (LMRP)) unexpectedly dropped approximately 15 feet while performing well completion operations on the King Embayment (KE) 002 Well in Mississippi Canyon Block 764. The BOP struck the wellhead, bent sideways, parted from the riser string at the riser adapter, and fell onto the seafloor. No environmental release or injuries were reported. The root cause was determined to be improper engagement of the BOP Landing Assist Tool (BLAT) in the BLAT riser spool.
key_phrases: BOP Landing Assist Tool (BLAT) not correctly engaged
load dogs extended in wrong profile area

threats.name: BLAT load dogs not fully extended and locked in correct profile
Inadequate weight control during tool installation due to active heave and rig movement
Procedure deviation - use of pipe handler instead of chain tongs
Loss of load support during suspended lift operation

consequences.name: Impact damage to wellhead and BOP
Structural damage to pipe handler, funnel guide, tubing head spool, tubing hanger, and riser joint
Financial loss and recovery operations required

controls.name: BLAT Load Dog Engagement and Locking Mechanism
controls.barrier_role: Mechanical load retention during suspended lift
controls.lod_basis: Primary mechanical barrier to prevent load drop

supporting_text: BSEE investigators did not observe scratch marks on the BLAT riser joint's designated 'load dog' profile area or on the key slot area. This would indicate the load dogs were not fully extended and locked inside the BLAT's riser joint load dog profile during the riser/BOP lift.

BSEE investigators observed scratch marks on the interior portion of the BLAT riser joint. The scratch marks were spaced and shaped in a way to resemble the BLAT's load dogs. The marks were located above the key slot area on the first tapered profile with a smaller ID above the key slot area. This indicates the load dogs were extended in the (wrong) upper profile area.
BSEE investigators observed the BLAT's load dogs were extended approximately 50% of the fully extended position.

controls.name: Transocean BLAT Installation Procedure
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control for correct tool installation and operation
controls.lod_basis: Procedural guidance to ensure proper BLAT engagement

supporting_text: The unlatch and hop plan was supported by Transocean's procedures for 'Utilizing BLAT (BOP Landing Assist Tool) to unlatch/latch BOP.'
Transocean's rig procedure, ('Utilizing BLAT to Latch/Unlatch') was not followed. The procedure indicated using two chain tongs to rotate the pipe, however, the pipe handler was used.

The procedure required 5,000 lbs to be set down on the BLAT profile while making the 8 CCW turns. However, the tool pusher noted the driller needed to chase the weight back and forth while the active heave drawworks was in the 'on' position. The 5,000 lb set down weight would be difficult to obtain in these conditions.

controls.name: Weight Control and Monitoring During Tool Installation
controls.barrier_role: Operational control to maintain proper load conditions during BLAT setup
controls.lod_basis: Procedural requirement to maintain 5,000 lb set-down weight during tool rotation

supporting_text: The driller set down 5,000 to 9,000 lbs of weight on the BLAT profile and turned the assembly 1/4 to 1/2 of a turn in the clockwise (CW) direction using the chain tongs.
Due to rig movement, the pipe handler was used to rotate the tool string counterclockwise (CCW). The driller rotated the tool string 8.5 turns CCW.
Weather conditions forced the driller to chase (pick up and down) the tool set-down weight while the active heave drawworks was in the 'on' position.

controls.name: Visual Inspection and Function Testing of BLAT Assembly
controls.barrier_role: Quality assurance check before subsea deployment
controls.lod_basis: Pre-operation verification to identify defects or installation issues

supporting_text: The well operation crew vertically suspended, function tested, and visually inspected the BLAT assembly on surface.

controls.name: Tensioner and Travel Block Load Distribution
controls.barrier_role: Load sharing mechanism to distribute suspended load safely
controls.lod_basis: Mechanical system to maintain controlled load during lift operations

supporting_text: The weight of the riser/ BOP/ LMRP string was predetermined to be split 40/60 with the tensioners carrying 40% and the travel block carrying 60% of the load.

The driller then slacked off to maintain a 30,000 to 50,000 lb overpull on the tool string. With the travel block maintaining an upward lifting force on the tool string of 30,000 to 50,000 lbs over the tool string weight.
The BOP descended abruptly on top of the wellhead.

controls.name: ROV Lock and Unlock Functions
controls.barrier_role: Subsea verification of BOP latching status
controls.lod_basis: Remote verification to confirm BOP engagement before lift

supporting_text: The remote operating vehicle (ROV) lock and un-lock functions were then performed.

controls.name: Hook Load Monitoring and Recording
controls.barrier_role: Detection and documentation of load changes during lift operations
controls.lod_basis: Real-time monitoring to detect abnormal load conditions

supporting_text: According the ROV video provided to BSEE, the hook load just prior to dropping the BOP was 1,161,610 lbs. The approximate hook load after dropping the BOP was 201,000 lbs. The differential between the two hook load weights (also considered the weight dropped) is 960,610 lbs.

controls.name: BOP/LMRP Recovery Operations
controls.barrier_role: Recovery and remediation of dropped equipment
controls.lod_basis: Post-incident recovery to mitigate environmental and operational consequences

supporting_text: On December 2, 2021, Shell started their BOP/LMRP recovery operations to retrieve the Deepwater Pontus BOP from the seafloor.

The recovery of the BOP required simultaneously jetting the soil below the bottom plate of the BOP (to break the suction effect of the sea floor) and applying 980,000 lbs of vertical pull utilizing a spear assembly.
On December 18, 2021, the BOP/LMRP was recovered to surface.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
